attributes.stem:
A 40-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department experiencing intermittent pain in his right upper abdomen. He works as a farmer. He initially noticed it whilst shearing his sheep several months ago, although the pain has since become more severe. He describes the pain as dull, and that he is also feeling a similar ache underneath his right shoulder. He is otherwise asymptomatic, and has not been experiencing any nausea, vomiting, or any changes to the colour of his skin. His medical history is unremarkable. He does not drink alcohol, smoke, or take recreational drugs. He has not recently travelled anywhere overseas.

On physical examination, his vital signs are within all the normal ranges. There is tenderness in his right upper quadrant, and some hepatomegaly. His cardiovascular and respiratory examinations are otherwise normal.

A CT-scan is performed and a representative section is shown (Image):
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presented with a several month history of dull right upper quadrant pain and right subscapular pain. The CT scan shows a multilocular cyst with areas of calcification in the cyst wall. These are the characteristic appearances of a hydatid cyst. This is most likely due to the risk factor of being a farmer making him more vulnerable to the parasite Echinococcus granulosus, which is endemic in rural areas with grazing cattle and sheep.

Hydatid cysts are due to the tapeworm parasite Echinococcus granulosus. The definitive hosts are dogs, in which the parasite resides in their intestinal tract. The eggs of the then contaminate soil and grass through the dog’s faeces. Cattle and sheep which share these environments with the dogs then ingest the eggs and become a secondary host. Human infection occurs when these eggs are ingested through the faecal-oral route, usually through contact with dogs. Humans can develop these hydatid cysts in their livers and lungs, which is usually asymptomatic in humans until the cysts enlarge to a sufficient size. If they become large in the liver, they can cause right upper quadrant pain, nausea, vomiting, or cause damage or obstruct the biliary tree. In the lungs, the cysts commonly cause cough, chest pain, dyspnoea, and haemoptysis.

Giant haemangiomas are giant hepatic venous malformations, a relatively uncommon non-neoplastic vascular lesion of the liver which can mimic tumours. These do not appear cystic on CT. They are typically asymptomatic, but can have symptoms related to any structures they compress (e.g. the biliary tree). On CT they typically appear as heterogeneously hypoattenuating masses with marked central areas of low attenuation

Focal nodular hyperplasia is a regenerative mass lesion located within the liver, and are usually asymptomatic, but can cause right upper quadrant discomfort. These are solid hypervascular lesions with a central scar.

Amoebic abscesses are a form of hepatic abscess resulting from Entamoeba histolyica infection. It is endemic to the Middle East and Eastern Asia. They typically present with general malaise, right upper quadrant pain, and frank sepsis. On CT they appear as rounded, well-defined lesions with an enhancing wall and peripheral zone of oedema.

Hepatocellular carcinoma is the most common primary malignancy of the liver, commonly associated with cirrhosis from alcohol or viral aetiologies. Clinically it can present with constitutional symptoms, jaundice, complications from portal hypertension, and hepatomegaly. On imaging they can have a variety of appearances, such as being massive (focal), nodular (multifocal), or infiltrative (diffuse). The lesions are typically solid and hypervascular, with varying degrees of vascular invasion. However, this is less likely in this patient as he has no risk factors for cirrhosis.
learningPoints: Whilst relatively uncommon, hydatid disease should always be considered when assessing a cystic lesion, particularly in the liver.
references: Arinc S, Kosif A, Ertugrul M, Arpag H, Alpay L, Ünal Ö, et al. Evaluation of pulmonary hydatid cyst cases. International Journal of Surgery. 2009;7(3):192–5. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19369124/
Frider B, Larrieu E, Odriozola M. Long-term outcome of asymptomatic liver hydatidosis. Journal of Hepatology. 1999;30(2):228–31. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10068100/

Gaillard F. Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Radiology reference article [Internet]. Radiopaedia Blog RSS. [cited 2021Jul29]. Available from: https://radiopaedia.org/articles/hepatocellular-carcinoma?lang=gb
Gaillard F. Hydatid disease: Radiology reference article [Internet]. Radiopaedia Blog RSS. [cited 2021Jul29]. Available from: https://radiopaedia.org/articles/hydatid-disease
